questions.q: How does the color of gold nanoparticles differ from bulk gold, and what causes this shift?
options: Nanoparticles are always colorless
Gold nanoparticles appear red/purple due to localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) at the nanoscale, while bulk gold is yellow due to band structure absorption
Nanoparticles are a darker shade of yellow
The color is caused by impurities
questions.explanation: LSPR—the collective oscillation of free electrons in metal nanoparticles—is tunable with size and shape. Particles 10-50 nm show strong LSPR in visible range, causing color changes. This phenomenon enables plasmonic applications.

questions.q: What is a critical difference between bulk and nanoparticle properties?
options: Only bulk materials are crystalline
Nanoparticles have much higher surface-to-volume ratio, making surface effects (e.g., catalysis, melting point) dominant
Nanoparticles cannot be measured
Bulk materials are always stronger
questions.explanation: Nanoparticles' enormous surface area per unit mass means surface atoms dominate properties. This causes lower melting points, higher reactivity, and enhanced catalytic activity compared to bulk forms.

questions.q: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are rolled graphene sheets. What structural feature gives them exceptional strength?
options: They contain more carbon atoms than graphite
Their sp² carbon bonding and seamless cylindrical structure provide high Young's modulus (>1 TPa) and tensile strength
They are hollow, reducing weight only
They are always filled with other materials
questions.explanation: Single-walled CNTs have Young's modulus exceeding 1 TPa—stronger than steel while far lighter. The continuous sp² bonding network with no grain boundaries delivers uninterrupted load transfer along the axis.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of quantum dots in optoelectronic applications?
options: They are cheaper to produce than bulk semiconductors
Size-tunable band gap allows emission wavelength control by adjusting nanocrystal size
They never require external power
They are visible without microscopy
questions.explanation: Quantum confinement in dots reduces effective band gap as size increases, shifting photoluminescence from UV to infrared. Narrow size distributions yield bright, tunable colors—enabling applications in displays and biological labeling.

questions.q: Nanoparticles often have melting points lower than bulk material. Why?
options: Nanoparticles are not actually solid
Reduced size increases surface energy fraction; surface atoms (higher energy) dominate, lowering overall melting point
Nanoparticles lack crystalline structure
Size has no effect on melting point
questions.explanation: The Gibbs-Thomson effect describes melting point depression in nanoparticles. As particle size shrinks, surface area increases relative to volume, raising the proportion of high-energy surface atoms. This lowers the equilibrium melting temperature.

questions.q: What is graphene, and why is it technologically significant?
options: It is thick graphite powder
A single layer of sp² hybridized carbon atoms forming a 2D honeycomb lattice with exceptional electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties
A type of plastic polymer
A naturally occurring mineral unchanged in nanotechnology
questions.explanation: Graphene is a true 2D crystal—one atom thick. Its massless Dirac fermions enable ballistic transport, it conducts heat exceptionally well, and its strength rivals CNTs. These properties promise applications in electronics and energy.

questions.q: In nanomaterial synthesis, the sol-gel method produces nanoparticles through which process?
options: Direct melting and cooling
Hydrolysis and polycondensation of metal alkoxides to form a gel, followed by drying and calcination to yield nanoparticles
Simple mechanical grinding
Electrochemical deposition at high voltage
questions.explanation: Sol-gel chemistry hydrolyzes alkoxides (e.g., Si(OEt)₄) into a sol, then condenses into a gel network. Controlled drying and heating produce oxides and ceramics as nanoparticles with tunable size and composition.

questions.q: How does doping affect the electrical properties of semiconductor nanoparticles?
options: Doping has no effect at nanoscale
Dopants introduce energy states within the band gap, shifting electrical conductivity; quantum confinement further modifies the effective band gap
Doping always makes nanoparticles insulating
Dopants only matter in bulk form
questions.explanation: In nanoparticles, dopants still donate/accept carriers, but quantum confinement shifts the band gap. The combined effect of doping and size control enables precise property tuning impossible in bulk materials.

questions.q: What is the significance of the surface-to-volume ratio in catalytic nanoparticles?
options: Higher ratio increases cost
Much higher ratio provides more active sites per unit mass, dramatically increasing catalytic turnover frequency and selectivity
Catalysis is independent of surface area
Small particles are always less catalytic
questions.explanation: Catalysis occurs at surfaces. Nanoparticles expose a huge fraction of atoms as active sites. A 10 nm gold particle has ~1 million times more surface area per gram than bulk gold, making nanoparticles efficient catalysts.

questions.q: Core-shell nanoparticles combine a nanoparticle core with a thin shell of different material. What advantage does this design offer?
options: It always makes particles larger and less useful
Shells can enhance stability, tune optical/magnetic properties, or improve biocompatibility without sacrificing core functionality
The shell reduces all particle properties
Core-shell is always unstable
questions.explanation: The shell isolates the core from environment (improving stability), engineers band structure (modifying optical properties), or adds biocompatibility. Designs like magnetic Fe₃O₄@Au combine iron oxide's magnetism with gold's biocompatibility and plasmonic properties.
